title: Drape-moulded bowl in air-dry clay
subtitle: A smooth curved dish shaped over an upturned bowl
excerpt: Drape moulding is the most direct route to a smooth, curved dish: a slab of clay is draped over an upturned bowl, takes its shape as it stiffens, and releases cleanly. No kiln or wheel is needed. The method produces a bowl whose exterior shape exactly matches the mould's outer curve. Air-dry clay makes this accessible in any kitchen.
sourceNotes: Drape-mould technique. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), ceramics entry. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: Draping a slab over a
drape mould

is the most direct way to produce a smooth, curved bowl without a wheel. An upturned salad bowl or mixing bowl is the mould; the clay slab is pressed over it and stiffens to the mould's shape. No kiln is needed. The finished bowl is decorative; air-dry clay is not waterproof.

attrs.body: Smooth ceramic or metal bowls work best. Rough plastic bowls can tear the clay on release. Choose a bowl with a gentle curve rather than steep, vertical sides: steep sides are harder to release cleanly.
attrs.title: Mould selection

content.text: What you need

items.name: Smooth upturned bowl (the mould), 20-25 cm diameter
Thin cotton cloth or cling film
Pottery rolling pin and 6 mm guide rails

content.text: Building the bowl
Step 1: Prepare the mould
Turn the bowl upside down. Drape a piece of cotton cloth or cling film over the outside, smoothing out all folds. A fold prints as a ridge in the finished bowl.
Step 2: Roll a slab larger than the mould
Roll a slab 6 mm thick and at least 5 cm wider in diameter than the mould. For a 22-cm mould, roll a slab at least 27 cm across. The extra allows the slab to drape down the sides to the rim level.
Step 3: Drape the slab over the mould

Fold the slab in half loosely, carry it to the mould, and lay the fold over the centre. Unfold carefully and let the slab settle under its own weight. Smooth from the centre outward with a damp sponge, pressing the clay gently against the mould surface without stretching it. Trim the edge with the fettling knife to a clean circle at the level you want the rim.
Step 4: Add surface texture if desired

While the clay is still soft, press a leaf, a lace offcut, or a textured stamp gently into the interior surface of the bowl. The impression will hold as the clay stiffens. Remove the texturing object before the clay reaches leather-hard.
Step 5: Release at leather-hard
Leave the draped slab on the mould for two to four hours until it reaches
leather-hard

. The clay will have pulled away slightly from the mould surface. Lift one edge of the cloth and the bowl will release cleanly. Set the bowl right-side-up on a flat board and leave to dry fully over one to two days.
Finishing

Sand any rough areas at bone-dry stage outdoors with 120-grit sandpaper. Seal with two coats of PVA diluted 1:1 with water. The sealed bowl makes a good fruit display dish or a key holder; do not use for water or fresh fruit for extended periods.
Variations
Use a dinner plate as the mould for a very shallow dish with a wide, flat base.
Cut a wavy or petal-edged rim with the fettling knife before the clay stiffens on the mould.
Press coloured slips into the interior before draping: the slip transfers to the clay surface and stays once dry.
Common problems

items.fix: Always use cloth or cling film. If stuck, wait another hour: the clay will release as it dries further.
items.cause: No release layer, or clay removed too early.
items.symptom: Bowl sticks to the mould and tears on release

items.fix: Fold the slab in half to find the centre before placing. Use the fettling knife to trim the rim level after the clay has stiffened.
items.cause: Slab was not centred on the mould before draping.

items.fix: Cover the rim with a strip of plastic while the rest of the bowl dries. Allow one to two days for complete drying before handling.
items.cause: The clay dried too quickly from the rim inward.
items.symptom: Cracks radiate from the rim inward
